cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure this out. The question is about what the author thinks regarding high school teachers. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to look at the part of the letter where the author talks about teachers. The relevant section is the third paragraph. Let me read that again. The author says: "My final issue I would like to call to your attention is teacher's salaries. The high school students of today are America's future. In a way, America's future depends greatly on the high school teachers of America. Saying this, it is sad that teacher's salaries are where they are. While teachers are working harder and harder every day to teach high school students, CEO's of big companies that are driving our countries economy into the ground are getting bonuses. I'm not saying that teacher's salaries should be the same of CEO's of companies, but I do strongly believe that action should be taken to increase the salaries of teachers because they have one of the most important jobs in America - - preparing our country for the future." So the author is talking about teacher salaries. The key points here are: 1. The author states that teacher salaries are not high enough ("it is sad that teacher's salaries are where they are"). 2. The author contrasts the effort of teachers with CEOs getting bonuses. 3. The author says they don't think teachers should have the same salary as CEOs, but they do believe action should be taken to increase teacher salaries. 4. The author emphasizes the importance of teachers' jobs in preparing the country's future. Looking at the options: A) The teachers' salaries are not high enough. This seems directly supported by the text. The author says "it is sad that teacher's salaries are where they are" and wants action to increase them. B) Teacher's salaries should be as high as CEO's. The author explicitly says "I'm not saying that teacher's salaries should be the same of CEO's of companies," so this is incorrect. C) The teachers work harder than CEO's of companies. The author mentions that teachers are working harder every day, and that CEOs are getting bonuses while the economy is struggling, but they don't directly compare the amount of work between teachers and CEOs. The focus is more on the disparity in rewards (bonuses vs. low salaries) rather than who works harder. So this might not be the best choice.
